derivation: # Hand-calculated derivation — zero-discount-rate identity case
When discount_rate_annual = 0, NPV degenerates to a plain sum of
(undiscounted) cashflows. This makes the math trivially auditable.
Setup: rate-and-term refi.
Old loan: $100,000 balance @ 7.00% APR, 60 months remaining.
New loan: $100,000 @ 5.00% APR, 60-month term.
Closing costs: $0 (no t=0 outflow).
Discount rate: 0.000000 (no time-value).
Analysis horizon: full new term (60 months).
Step 3: Monthly savings = old - new = $1,980.12 - $1,887.12 = $93.00 / month.
Step 4: Over 60 months at 0% discount: NPV = 60 * $93.00 = $5,580.00 exactly.
Step 5: Simple breakeven = ceil($0 / $93.00) = 0 (zero closing costs).
Status = 'zero_costs'.
Step 6: NPV-based breakeven: smallest n where cumulative NPV >= 0.
Since closing_costs = 0, the period-0 cashflow is empty and the
cumulative NPV is exactly $0.00 at n=0 — already satisfying >=0.
So npv_months = 0, status = 'ok' (the project's _compute_breakeven_npv
scans starting at n=0; see lib/refinance.py _compute_breakeven_npv).
Engine assertion: exact Decimal equality on npv, monthly_savings,
old_monthly_pi, new_monthly_pi.
